content.body: This document exists to define first-class mapping objects between current UX and desired UX. Use it when a database, admin UI, refactor plan, or audit needs to show exactly how existing behavior relates to intended behavior. For broader documented-current, observed-current, intended, desired, governing, pending, superseded, or drift relationships, use behavior-thread parent containers and relationship mappings.

content.title: Purpose
body: - Which current behavior corresponds to which desired behavior?
- Is it preserved, changed, added, removed, split, merged, or unresolved?
- Does a user need to confirm the change?

content.title: Behavior Lifecycle Mapping Rule
body: Mappings should expose whether a behavior is observed runtime, documented current, verified current, intended target, intended change, ideal future, out-of-scope candidate, runtime drift, or pending decision.
If a mapping discovers behavior that is not yet owned by an App Behavior row or behavior-thread container, route it to the correct App Behavior destination or record a bounded gap.
A mapping may describe ideal/future behavior, but it must mark it non-current and outside current scope unless explicit authority expands scope.
Proof and closeout should use mappings to show which behavior was preserved, changed, promoted, deferred, or left pending.

content.title: Central Row Reference Rule
body: Current-to-desired mappings must reference central App Behavior row IDs and paths when they govern planning, implementation, proof, review, or closeout.
A local mapping or cached snapshot may orient the agent, but the central App Behavior row remains the governing behavior record.

When a mapping marks behavior as `replaced`, the default lifecycle is `supersede_then_archive_or_remove_after_proof`: supersede first with replacement refs and proof obligations, then archive or remove after proof-backed closeout.

content.title: First-Class Objects
body: Do not rely on loose inline references as the authoritative relationship. Store current-to-desired relationships as `uxCurrentDesiredMapping` items.

content.title: Many-To-Many Rule
body: Even a one-to-one mapping should use arrays so split and merge cases do not need a different shape.

content.title: Confirmation Rule
content.body: Behavior changes should include recommendation, confidence, alternatives, consequences, and confirmation status. If a desired behavior changes existing UX and is not confirmed, implementation should be blocked or limited to non-governing preparation work.
